Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Welcome to The Makepeace Business System!
This is an exciting moment.
You’re about to discover the secrets that have made Clayton Makepeace one of the most
sought-after copywriters and direct response marketers in the world – Secrets that will
show you how you can be more successful and make more money than you ever thought
possible.
We’re not talking obscure theories that may or may not produce results. What Clayton
reveals is a proven system … one you can easily adapt to your own business and style …
that has produced real, measurable results time after time.
One that will enable you to land your dream clients … produce bigger winners, more often
… and collect those big royalty checks you’ve always dreamed about!
On these first two CDs, seasoned copywriter and marketer Tony Flores gets Clayton
to introduce The Makepeace Business System and explain why it’s so different from
everything out there … why it works … and why you’d have to be certifiably insane not to
consider adopting it for yourself.
If you like, you can print out the transcript to keep handy while you’re listening to the
interview. You’ll find it on the Bonus CD in your case, along with all the other transcripts,
PDFs, and special reports.
Now, let’s join Tony and Clayton …
Clayton: 	Hello
Tony: 	Hey Clayton
Clayton: 	Hey Tony, how you doing?
Tony: 	How’s it going? We’ll, it’s good to have you.
As you know, the reason I had you give me a call today is to discuss this
model that you’ve been writing about.
I’ve prepared a series of what I believe to be very tough questions and really
getting into more of the specifics and some of the things that I’m just not
sure of and haven’t seen with my own two eyes, so I’d like to hear more from
you about it.

-- 1 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Since I have 500 friends listening in to the call right now, why don’t you begin
with a quick explanation of what and how it works.
Clayton: 	Sure. Essentially, most copywriters make money in one of three ways.
Actually there are four ways in all and I’ll just go through them all.
The first one is you can get a job. You go through a course. You get your
chops and you apply for a job. Maybe it’s with an ad agency or a company
that does direct response. Len Bailey is on the phone with us today. Until he
came to work for us he was writing copy for a major coin company in New
Hampshire.
There are agencies. I hired a guy out of a radio station once and mentored
him. Now he owns a multi-million dollar company in New Orleans.
Anyway, you can get these kinds of jobs. They’re really great incubators,
especially when you’re starting out. I did that myself.
Yahoo says that the average salary for a copywriter in America is around
$90,000. I’m assuming that they weren’t polling people like me and Gary
Bencivenga and Jim Rutz when they came up with that number. It’s more
likely that they were polling people who had regular jobs as copywriters.
So you know if you rise to the top in a major agency you’ll make much more
than that, definitely into the six figures. But if you’re just starting out you may
start at $40,000 or $35,000 or $30,000.
That’s one way to go. If you’re into job security and if you don’t mind going to
work every day and dealing with a boss and everything, that’s one way to do
it.
The way that most people, including AWAI, refer to copywriting is in terms of
freelancing. Most freelancers just work out of their home and they write for a
lot of different clients during the year.
Those copywriters range from six figures up to a million dollars a year in
income. They receive their income from a combination of advances, flat
fees, and royalties on the work that they do. The royalty writers, obviously,
are the ones at the upper end of that scale.
There are several downsides to freelancing though. There’s only so much
time in the year that you can set aside for writing. Each time you go to work
on a new product or for a new client, you have to go through a learning

-- 2 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 3 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
curve. You have to learn about the product, the market, what they’ve tried
before, what worked, what didn’t. That takes time and you don’t get paid for
that time. That’s part of the writing process.
You also have to spend time selling yourself to new clients all the time.
When I was freelancing I was able to do maybe an average of eight
promotions a year. I would schedule myself out in advance and I had to
spend time sending emails out to potential clients.
I had to spend time with them on the phone and schmoozing and doing all
of that stuff that none of us really enjoy doing, which takes even more time
away from writing.
Tony: 	Yeah, you’ve got to hoof it a little bit.
Clayton: 	Yeah, you’ve got to hustle. The fact is, I got to the point where I enjoyed it
over time. But especially at first it was intimidating and frustrating, especially
when you’re under the gun and need to finish a project or generate some
money to spend this month. Instead you’re banging the phones trying to find
work for next month. It can be pretty disruptive and frustrating.
The third drawback is that every client you work with knows that next month
you’ll be working for another client and in many cases a direct competitor of
theirs.
So the chance that they’re going to give you strategic information that they
consider to be their company’s crown jewels about what they tested or tried,
what lists are going to their prospect profiles, their average sale or things like
that are fairly limited. Over time they may learn to trust you and you may get
more information.
As recently as two years ago I was meeting with a client that I had been
doing business with for 15 years and I asked a question about one of their
promotions. This was right after they said, “Oh, we’ll tell you anything. We
trust you.”
I asked a question about one of their promotions and there was this long
silence and the Creative Director said, “We’re not at liberty to share that.”
Regardless of what they tell you, they are not giving you all of the
information that you need to create the biggest possible winner.
Then finally, there’s competition on each assignment. It really comes in

-- 3 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
two ways. Number one is it’s an established procedure that when these
companies hire a copywriter to beat a control, they quite often will hire two or
three writers at a time, then mail all three of those writers’ efforts against the
control in a future mailing.
So instead of just trying to beat the control, which is a known quantity – you
can look at it and say, “Okay, here are the weak points and here are the
places I would beat it,” -- you’re also up against the unknown quantity of
one, two, or even three new packages by other copywriters.
Depending on where you are in your career, it would be pretty daunting
to find out, especially after the fact as it often happens, that while you
thought you were going up against a single control in which you had spotted
weaknesses, you were also unbeknownst to you going up against Arthur
Johnson or Jim Rutz or Parris Lampropoulos on that project, so your odds of
winning are obviously much lower.
Then even if you’re lucky enough to get the control in a situation like that,
that client next month is going to go out and hire Arthur Johnson, Jim Rutz,
Parris Lampropoulos or Carline Anglade-Cole or another top writer to try to
beat your control to get a better response rate, average sale, and return on
investment.
So that means that even when you get the control, there’s a very good
chance that the amount of royalties you’ll earn from that control will be very
limited. Every time they re-mail it or they use it online, they’ll be testing you
against someone else.
The third option, which is really kind of what I fell into back in the 1980’s,
is what I call partnering. It differs from freelancing because it focuses on
creating a deeper relationship with just one or two clients.
It eliminates the learning curve. It eliminates the time you waste selling
yourself during the year. It gives you full access to the company’s crown
jewels. It eliminates competition because you have a two-way exclusive with
that client and it allows you to create up to seven streams of income from a
single client.
That model, combined with what I think is some fairly decent copywriting, is
what has got my income range up to four times higher than my competitors,
than other A-level writers.
We still don’t know for sure yet because I haven’t seen my tax returns, but

-- 4 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
I’m pretty sure that last year we did right around $4 million. Maybe we did
slightly less, maybe slightly more, but around $4 million in royalties.
That’s the difference. It was easier for me because I was only dealing with
a fraction of the number of personalities I otherwise would have had to deal
with.
I was able to focus on one product and get to know the product and the
market intimately. I didn’t have to spend one minute during the year selling
myself to anybody or filling my schedule.
I had advantages that no freelancer has in terms of protection from
competition and full data, past test data, and everything the client knew
about his market.
By far the biggest money you can make, of course though, isn’t partnering.
It’s really the next step. That would be building your own business, selecting
a product and taking it to the moon yourself. 	That way, instead of
making a percentage of revenues and profits, you make 100% of the profits.
It’s a great way to go.
The other day on our blog, the guy that invented TradeStation I think it was
– no, it was Investools – he got on there and he said, “Makepeace is right
about partnering, but there is a better way.”
You know, he’s right in a way. Building your own business is a better way.
That’s how you get to be Billy Mays or Guthy-Renker or Ron Popeil or Joe
Sugarman.
Reality intervenes. First of all, although your income potential is unlimited,
so is the downside. The direct marketing world is littered with the corpses of
people who tried this and didn’t have enough capital or weren’t really able to
pull out a big winner.
Building your own business is easier online now where the costs are lower,
but you still have to eat while you’re building the company so you have to
have some capital reserves there.
I think for most of the people that read The Total Package and most of the
people that I talk to, partnering is by far the most potentially profitable and
also the most realistic way to begin ramping up your income right now today.
So that’s the basic concept.

-- 5 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Tony: 	You described basically how you make up to four times more than a lot
of the other very high level freelancers, but how does that work out for
the business owner? Why would he agree to give out that much more
compensation?
Clayton: 	It sounds like it would be a pretty tough sell. You walk in and you say, “I
know you’re paying your freelancers $1 million a year right now, but if you
hire me exclusively and promise to never hire them again, you’ll only have to
pay me $4 million.”
But it really doesn’t work that way. I’ve said this so many times. First of all,
the scarcest resource every direct response business owner has is great
copywriting.
If you try to get on Arthur Johnson’s dance card, chances are he’ll just
probably tell you no. If he does say yes, his closest writing slot could be six
months, a year, or even more from now. The same is true with Parris and
Carline and all of these other top freelance copywriters.
That’s a major problem for a company that needs to increase its customer
base today or maximize revenues from that customer base today or to take
advantage of an opportunity or launch a new product that’s going to be
ready next month or even the month after.
So having instant access to a competent copywriter is money in the bank for
a direct response company. It solves a major problem that they have right
now.
The second reason is that right now the companies that you’re talking to
have a pool of ideas for products and promotions that have been generated
by their staff or by the company owner himself.
When a direct marketing pro, a copywriter who understands marketing nuts
and bolts, is added to the mix, somebody who’s really gone to school on
copywriting in offers and headlines and the products that are working best in
the mail and online right now and so forth –
When somebody walks through the door with that capacity and adds to it
an intimate understanding of the client’s company and his product and his
market, the pool of great ideas is going to increase exponentially. In other
words, you bring a lot to the table. Great ideas are really the fuel that runs
direct marketing companies, so having an exclusive with a company and
being completely committed to that company does that.

-- 6 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Also, I have more great ideas for my clients in the shower in the morning
than I do when I come to work and sit down at my desk. The thing is that
when you’re committed to a client you’re constantly thinking about that
client. Everything else you see is in the context of that client’s company.
If you see a new piece of software or new web technology or a new format
for a direct mail piece, that client is going to get that idea and he knows that
you’re not going to take it down the road to his competitor.
Then third, there are more opportunities that can be seized. Like I said,
every direct response company out there is like a giant sieve and it’s just like
it’s got money pouring out the bottom of it because of all of the holes in its
marketing plan.
Having the horsepower of a dedicated copywriting marketer on board gives
you the opportunity to patch those leaks and to realize that revenue and
those profits that you’re losing right now.
Every company that you talk to is going to say, “We’ve got more ideas than
Carter has liver pills, but our problem is execution. We can’t find enough
copywriters. We can’t find enough marketing people to take advantage of
them.”
As we just said, you’re going to come in with even more ideas so there’s
huge growth potential there.
Tony: 	Clayton, I want to rewind for a second. You mentioned a couple things
during the seizing opportunities and better results and profits and everything.
In order for me to really believe that, especially to believe that across the
board, I think I need a little more details or specifics. I’m not totally convinced
of why that is. Could you go into that just a little bit more so that I know
exactly what you mean?
Clayton: 	Sure. Both in terms of better ideas and more opportunities, one of the
things that I think copywriters should do when they’re dealing with a client
who, let’s say, is creating a new customer acquisition promotion, as part of
your “research” for that, it’s very possible to ask, “I’d really like to see what
happens to the new customers that I’m going to produce for you. What will
their experience be and how they need to be set up for secondary sale and
so forth.”

-- 7 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 8 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
While you’re doing that you’re going to see all kinds of situations in the
company that aren’t being addressed.
For example, in a $100 million dollar direct marketing company I was
working with, this comes under the better ideas banner, I’d been working
with this company for a couple of months. Just on a whim I thought, “You
know, my direct mail is working pretty well, but I wonder whether customer
service is helping or hurting me.”
So I picked up the phone and I called the toll free number and I said, “I want
to subscribe to your newsletter.”
The woman said, “Okay, how long of a subscription would you like?”
I said, “Well, what do you have?”
She said, “Well, one year is $99.00 and two years is $189.00.”
I said, “Well, okay, so one year is slightly cheaper on a monthly basis or it’s
about the same price, so what’s to keep me from just subscribing for $99.00
and subscribing again next year for the same amount if I like it?”
She said, “Nothing.”
So I said, “Do I get my money back if I subscribe for two years?”
She said, “Well, yeah.” I had to drag it out of her.
Finally I said, “You know what? This sounds really good, but you’ve told me
I can only subscribe for one or two years, I think I’d like to try three years.
What’s your offer on that?”
My hand to God, her answer was, “I’m sorry sir. We don’t offer three year
subscriptions.”
Well, that resulted in an email that was fired off in about 30 seconds to
the company owner that said, “Hey, you know what? Here’s an idea, why
don’t you get some smarter customer service people or at least script them
better?”
So we did that and we saw a noticeable increase in average unit of sale on
our direct mail promotions as a result.

-- 8 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
More opportunities seized. Another company I walked into we were doing
great customer acquisition promotions, but I started noticing when I was
looking at his RFA tables, which for those of you who don’t know are simply
a spreadsheet that shows how many of his customers are at various levels
of activity in the company. One of them is their recency of purchase metric.
The recency of purchase metric tells you how many people have ordered
within the last 30 days, 31 to 60 days and so forth, going all the way back.
The company was defining an active customer as somebody who had
ordered within the last 12 months.
So I took a look at that and I said, “Well, do you realize that you’re going to
lose 20% of your active customer base in the next 60 days because they’re
either at 11 or 12 months since their last purchase?”
The company owner said, “Yeah, I know that. We’ve been meaning to get
around to do a customer retention program but we just don’t have the time
and we don’t have the copywriters available to do that for us.” Here a fifth of
his company was about to vanish into thin air.
So being committed to the company would enable you to say, “Look, don’t
worry about that. We can’t let this happen. I will write a promotion or a series
to retain those customers. We’ll keep them on board.
“We’ll turn them from 12 month customers into one month customers on
their recency metric and you’ll retain them so that you’ll have net growth this
month instead of a net loss in terms of active customers.”
Tony: 	You’re saying that you’re motivated to do that because you’re on a
percentage deal with that client, whereas if you’re just getting paid by the job
then you don’t really care.
Clayton: 	As far as the deal that you strike for that kind of a promotion, there’s any
number of ways you could go.
Let’s say for example that you just have a very simple deal with the client
where you’re doing his new customer acquisition promotions for a royalty
of say $20.00 or $30.00 per thousand pieces mailed or online a royalty
of 5 - 10% of revenues. In addition you’re also doing back-end monthly
promotions to existing customers, right? This is another big advantage to
business owners by the way.
If you’re doing the back-end promotions, you don’t have to charge an arm

-- 9 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 0 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
and a leg to do anything that is either going to retain customers or increase
his customer base because the bigger that base is, the more money you’re
going to make because you’re doing the promotions to that base and you’re
taking a percentage of sales.
It gives you the flexibility as a writer to say, which I do quite often as a
matter of fact, “Hey don’t worry about this one. I’ll do it free,” because within
30 days my income is going to jump 20 or 30% once we implement this
retention program. So it gives you a lot more flexibility there too.
Of course business owners love getting anything they’re not paying for.
Don’t we all, right? Then the bottom line really is better results, faster growth
and greater profits. 	The key is, of course, demonstrating and creating
personal proof for the business owner that you can indeed deliver those
things.
If he has instant access to a copywriter to take advantage of opportunities,
if he’s got a bigger pool of better ideas because you’re involved, and if he’s
able to seize more opportunities that he’s missing right now – and believe
me, as a company owner when you see those opportunities month after
month not being capitalized on, it’s torture. It’s horrible. That’s money right
off your bottom line. So that’s a huge benefit. Those are just some of the
reasons.
I might also say, as more and more people become aware of this model and
business owners look around at companies, including in some cases their
competitors who are partnering, and they see the results that partnering
produces, I think you’re going to see company owners actively seeking and
recruiting copywriter marketers as partners.
Tony: 	Well, you know, Clayton, a lot of what you say makes sense, but I worry that
it makes sense for you and not necessarily for me or I think a lot of the other
people that are listening on the call.
You obviously have a very extensive track record that you can do all these
things. You’re a famous copywriter and you’re already heavily in demand.
Obviously a client sees green when he’s talking to you, whereas that may
not be the case for somebody like me or some of the other people on our list
at this stage.
So how does that work if you’re not famous or if you don’t have any
monster winners under your belt, like where you’ve generated a million new
customers and all that kind of stuff? What is the regular guy to do?

-- 10 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Clayton: 	Well, before I get to that, let me just rewind for a second as you said.
You asked a question about specific examples of how the partnership model
can really benefit company owners and major opportunities that aren’t fully
exploited. I gave you specific ones from my actual experiences. Let me just
give you a couple more real quick, okay?
Number one, the company owner is going to get more effective new
customer acquisition promotions because instead of working with a writer
who has a passing interest in the company and who is just learning about
the company and its products and prospects, he now has somebody who’s
eating, sleeping, and breathing customer acquisition for him.
He has someone who’s intimately aware and grows more intimately aware
with every month with every new promotion of what’s working and what isn’t.
Secondly, there’s the opportunity to go in and look at things like his product
stuffers and thank you promotions. I’m very big on every single customer
contact is an opportunity to do two things: bond with the customer and sell
something. Whether the sell is a bump or a cross-marketing promotion,
that’s an area where most companies are doing a really horrible job.
When they deliver their product, what’s in it to set up a secondary sale? Do
they do a thank you letter that says, “Oh, by the way, I’m going to give you
an extra 30 days to increase your subscription from a one year to a two
year.”
There are tons of opportunities there: discount coupons, calendars with
discount coupons, catalogs, and so forth.
By the way, for copywriters there’s nothing sweeter than to write a website or
a catalog in 2005, and in 2008 because it’s standing copy you’re still getting
royalties on it.
More effective welcome aboard and gauntlet series – We call gauntlets the
first 30 to 60 days typically that a new customer is on file. During that period
of time, a concerted effort to bond with the customer and to generate a
secondary sale does miracles for the company.
Number one, they can allocate some of that money to their new customer
acquisition campaigns, which allows them to be very aggressive in the mail
or on TV or radio or any media they want.

-- 11 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
They know that even though they may not get 100% of cost back on that
initial promotion, once they have one of these kinds of programs in place
for a period of time, they can count on secondary sales being at a certain
level within the first 30, 60 or 90 days. It’s a very powerful tool for exploding
company’s customer bases.
Then there are more effective promotions to existing customers. Again, now
you know these customers. They’re your friends. You’re able to structure
polls and surveys and even focus groups with them.
You get to know what they want and what kinds of offers that they prefer and
when they’re most likely to order. You’re able to segment the file and do both
vertical and horizontal promotions to them.
So that instead of everybody on the file receiving the same promotion at
the same time, you’ll do one promotion in the first week of the month that’s
based on a shared experience like a holiday; Christmas, Easter, Halloween
or whatever. Those are the vertical promotions.
Then the next week you can do a horizontal promotion where you address
each group of customers on the basis of some shared quality. For instance,
use their recency of order, their frequency of order, the average size of their
order, their months on file and so forth.
By slicing and dicing a customer file that way, you maximize your revenue
from every single segment of the file. It’s a multiplying effect. The growth and
revenues profits can just be staggering.
I mentioned more effective customer retention. Another one is testing. Every
company out there feels guilty as hell because they don’t test enough.
They know they should be testing more. They know they should be testing
headlines, copy, offers, and media.
There are a hundred things to test and they’re not doing it because they
don’t have the horsepower. They don’t have the copywriters to create the
test panels. Being a copywriter marketer who can not only come in and
create test panels, but can also help strategize what we should be testing
now, what’s most important to test on our current control, again that’s
another quantum growth driver for a company.
Those are just some of the other things I wanted to cover in terms of
specifics.

-- 12 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 3 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Tony, that’s your tremendous value to The Total Package. You force us to
be specific. That was the whole idea behind the Screaming Eagle and I still
think it was one of the most brilliant ideas I’ve heard.
Tony: 	Thank you, and I had asked you a question and you went back and we can
get to that in a second, but one of the things that I feel like I hear you saying
also with this, and I think it’s partially an answer to a question but not directly,
is that part of this process is the appeal.
Why it works is not so much because of the copywriter. It’s because of the
process, the system. The system creates advantage. I think as copywriters
we tend to think that we do everything. Maybe in this case the system is
producing its results on top of the copywriter’s skill. Is that what you’re
saying?
Clayton: 	That’s correct. The fact of the matter is that if you can go in and create
a decent response in some of these areas, not even world beating, but
you’re bringing him more online with more tests and customer retention and
bonding with existing customers which results in greater sales down the
road and more retention down the road too – if you’re doing these things
even with okay copy, you can see a multiplying effect in the company.
Another point here, if you’re a copywriter marketer and you’re talking to
a business owner about this, remember you’re first and foremost a sales
person. What does a sales person do? What is the most important thing
for a sales person to be aware of? The dominant resonant emotions of his
prospect.
Now your prospect is a company owner. How is he feeling about his current
situation? I haven’t met the business owner yet who wasn’t frustrated that he
wasn’t growing faster and feeling guilty that he wasn’t doing a better job of
testing and agonizing over his cancellation rates or his low renewal rates.
Those are the drivers that really put the business owner on your side in the
first place. If you can go in and show him how together as a team the two of
you can take care of these problems and incrementally improve each one of
them, the bottom line to the business owner is huge.
Tony: 	Okay, now let’s get back to what I was asking in terms of what a regular guy
does, somebody who’s not famous and doesn’t have a track record that’s
been blasted across the internet and all that kind of stuff.

-- 13 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
We talked about it a little bit I think in the sense that you’re selling the
process and not just selling yourself. You’re selling the process itself.
Tell me a little bit more about that. I really wonder how eager a business
owner is going to be to jump on a deal like this if he hasn’t heard of the
person or they don’t have a list of winners a mile long.
Clayton: 	Right. Again, this is like a first date.
I’ll give you an example that I know you’re familiar with. Let’s say you’re
going out on a first date with a gal and you immediately show her a picture
of what you hope is the outcome of that date, which could be anything from
blissfully smoking a cigarette in the afterglow, or marriage and children
– each of which could be equally terrifying on a first date. [laughing]
I think that would be a fairly major blunder. I think it’s the same way with the
company that you’re dealing with.
My buddy Rich Schefren is always making a big deal about the three levels
of proof. There’s objective third party proof where you in a direct mail piece
for example, you’ll cite Bloomberg as the authority on a particular fact or
point that you’re trying to make. That’s third party. “So and so says this.”
Then there’s social proof, which is far more powerful, and that’s when a peer
tells your prospect that he should buy your product.
Then there’s personal proof, where your prospect himself experiences the
power of what you’re trying to get him to do.
Not only do you go in armed with understanding the dominant emotions
of the company owner that you want to partner with, but you also go in
understanding that showing him a mental image of the two of you blissfully
having that smoke or where you hope this thing will ultimately go could be a
mistake.
Secondly, the best way to proceed is to provide personal proof. Let him
sample the goods.
Sometimes I actually call it a first date when I’m talking to a new client. At
this point in my career quite often they’ll begin talking to me about partnering
and building a long-term exclusive relationship.
Quite often I will tell them, “No, you know what, let’s not. Instead let’s

-- 14 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
test drive each other. Let’s have a first date. Why don’t we do a project
together? If I like the amount of money I make and you like the amount of
money you make and we both like working with each other, then we’ll talk
about the next step.”
I think it’s really the same no matter who you are or where you are in your
career. Moving from freelancing into partnering really should be a natural
process. It should be a sales process on either party’s part.
You want to know that you enjoy working together. You want to both feel that
the process of creating the promotion was pleasant, stimulating and fun. You
both need to feel the outcome was mutually satisfying.
I prefer it that way. I also advise my friends, “Look, just do a project or two
together.” If the business owner is fairly unsuspecting and he basically thinks
he’s hiring a freelancer, that’s fine, too.
During that process be sure and ask questions that allow you to make
suggestions that demonstrate and provide personal proof to your
prospective partner that you bring tremendous value to the table – a value
way beyond simply writing copy.
If you do that then you don’t need to be famous. You don’t need to have your
own online ezine. Although they never hurt to have, you don’t need to. You
don’t need to have a hundred winners under your belt. What you have done
for other people in the past is far less salient to this perspective partner than
what you did for him last month. That’s what’s going to ultimately not just
convince a potential partner, but have a potential partner pursue you.
I’ve told this story before. You know sometimes, at some point after three
years of writing The Total Package I’ve told every story I have four or five
times. Eventually I’m going to get to the point where I hold up a card with a
number on it and everybody will know which story I’m referring to, like the
guy whose wife has heard all the jokes before.
When I started working with Security Rare Coin I was doing customer
acquisition. It included direct mail pieces, 16 – 24 sales page letters in 6 x 9
envelopes.
We were doing really well. His subscriber following was growing like crazy
and he was very happy with that. So he had personal proof that maybe this
Clayton does know what he’s talking about.

-- 15 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
He then approached me. He said, “Tell you what, I don’t want to work with
other copywriters and I don’t want you to work other investment financial
companies. Why don’t we have an exclusive?”
Since at the time I really didn’t have anyone else I was working with, it was
fine by me. So we set up an arrangement on that basis so I could focus on
his customer acquisition.
Then one day in December I said, “Hey Bill, what do you do with these new
customers after they come on board?”
He said, “They get their newsletter every month and there’s a little stuffer in
there that offers them a rare coin that they can buy. That’s where we really
make our money.”
I asked if I could see some of those promotions. He said sure. I called him
up on the phone and said, “Bill, these suck! These are really stinking up the
joint. I think I can do a lot better.”
He said, “Well okay, but they’re working pretty good. If you think you can,
why don’t you write the insert for January?” So I agreed to do that. It was a
two-page insert.
He wanted to know how much I would charge him and I told him nothing
because I just wanted to see if I could do it. He said okay. There’s no better
price than free. I guess I could have offered to pay him for the privilege of
writing it, but I didn’t have to because he jumped at free.
I wrote the piece and it literally took me a day – less than one day if I
remember right. I turned in the copy and he said, “Oh, this is different than
what we’re doing now.”
I said, “You expected the same? Mail it. I went to the trouble to write it so
mail the thing.” He decided fine, he would.
Long story short, his sales jumped 1,000% in 30 days. He had been doing
$360,000 a month in sales and we did $3.6 million in January based on that
one insert.
At that point I didn’t have to pitch anything to the guy. He was on the phone
to me saying he wanted me to move there and take over all his marketing.
He went on and on.

-- 16 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
I said, “Bill, you can’t afford me.” At the time I was making $90,000 a year.
This was 27 years ago and I was saying you can’t afford me.
He said, “Try me.”
My response was, “Okay. First of all, if I’m going to move my family…”
which I was willing to do, but you don’t have to do this. All my clients are long
distance. At the time I was looking for a change anyway.
“If I’m going to move my family up there, first of all you have to pay all our
moving expenses.”
He said, “Done.”
“I want a $40,000 bonus because I’m going to have to cancel projects with
other clients.”
He said, “Done.”
“I want $250,000 a year base pay, retainer.”
He said, “Done.”
“I want 5% of sales.”
He said, “So when can you be here?”
I said, “Oh, did I mention a company car, a Cadillac or better?”
He goes, “When can you be here?”
Of course, at that point I’m kicking myself because I figured I could have
probably asked for double.
Tony: 	You didn’t make him sweat enough.
Clayton: 	Exactly. My point is that it was a natural process. I demonstrated my value
first and then it wasn’t a matter of selling him it was simply a matter of setting
the deal.
Tony: 	One of the things I’m curious about that doesn’t quite make sense to me in
writing the newsletter every month is this. I get the opportunity to network
with a lot of the other top marketers and writers in our industry on the

-- 17 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 8 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
internet and they are some very sharp people obviously.
One of the things that makes me wonder is if this is such a superior
model way of working – a relationship model for both entrepreneurs and
copywriters – why aren’t more of the established top writers doing it?
Clayton: 	Change is tough but more and more of them are. A few years ago I helped
Parris Lampropoulos set up an exclusive partnering relationship with
Soundview, Chip Wood’s group down in Atlanta.
Last year Jim Punkre, who’s one of the top writers in the health field, called
me up and asked me to help him set up a partnering arrangement with Early
to Rise.
Rich Schefren was desperately in need of copywriting help. Last month I put
him together with a friend of mine, Bob Hutchinson. I kind of kibitzed from
the sidelines to help them structure a partnering relationship.
Did you meet Bob at Rich’s event, Tony?
Tony: 	I did. He is honestly a great guy and a hell of a copywriter.
Clayton: 	He is really good. Those are guys I have helped set up those things. All of
those are A level writers.
In addition to them, since I began writing about this in January, if you go to
the archives and look at the comments of each issue you’ll see that there
are other copywriters coming out of the woodwork with stories about how
they’re partnering.
Some of them are in response to the articles we’ve done. Others have fallen
into this naturally like I did with SRC. More and more people are doing it.
I’m doing my best and almost feel like a union organizer here. I’d like to
organize all of the companies out there to say they don’t want to work with
just freelancers, they want partners.
I want the copywriters to say, “Who needs freelancing? I want to partner with
the companies I work with.” I think as word gets out more people are going
to be doing it and we’ll see the model changing.
Tony: 	Another concern that I have is this. Let’s say that you do go through this
process. You have that first date and are able to convince that client to

-- 18 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
commit to them to take on a bigger, broader relationship.
I think the biggest fear I would have, and I think a lot of the other writers that
would initiate this process would have, is failing. What if this doesn’t work
out? What if you can’t produce across the board the improvement you are
essentially promising the client when you initiate the discussion?
I wonder how you know when you’re ready, especially in terms of knowledge
and skill to approach somebody with a partnership deal. At what point do
you really know?
Clayton: 	Obviously no approach to building a copywriting business is going to work
unless your product is there and works. It’s just like any other company. If
your product stinks you’re going to have a rough go of it.
If you can write good, solid sales copy that creates even incremental
increases in response in average sale for a client, you already have the
basic tools to begin doing this.
The natural progression is this. Your potential partner is thinking of you as
a copywriter anyway. All you’re doing is saying, “You’re doing the customer
acquisition promos now, so let me take a look at what you do with the
customers after they come on board.”
That’s a copywriting function. It’s not marketing consultation or grand
strategy. It’s not even execution and procedures. It’s still copy. If you have
that, you can begin doing that now. It’s just as easy as what I said with SRC.
I got into that very lucrative new contract with them entirely on the basis of
copy.
Later on I built an entire marketing department for him. I hired 35 graphic
artists to handle the quantity of promotions we were doing our house file.
Remember, that was back in the days when we still had to cut and paste
type on an art board with rubber cement. We needed 35 artists plus a bunch
of copywriters and marketing people and everything else.
I built an entire marketing department for him and got heavily involved in
other aspects of the company. That came later.
First ,you key on what you can do well now and just find additional ways to
apply it to that business owner’s company. That alone is enough to make
you a lot of money.

-- 19 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 0 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Later on, of course, as working with a customer you’re going to find things
that need fixing. You’re going to figure out that there is other media that
would work better.
Maybe he should try a multiple step sales strategy instead of a single step.
Maybe he doesn’t have an adequate product price ladder. You want a ladder
or pyramid where at the bottom rung you’ve got a product that’s priced
attractively for people who have never heard of you before, that won’t be an
obstacle to them becoming customers in the first place.
At the other end of the ladder you need products that will allow you to
maximize revenues from each existing customer. You need incremental
steps along the way, so maybe you’ll identify opportunities there.
Maybe you’ll see problems on the staff. Maybe you’ll see lack of
infrastructure. Maybe you’ll see new tools or programs that need to be
bought. Maybe you’ll dabble in strategy by offering an offer test or new ways
to bump a new customer on the first purchase, or new ways to introduce
them to different product lines that the company has.
Those things will happen naturally as you work together. You will find that
your role naturally expands within the company.
I think what’s desperately needed today is for copywriters to develop a
broader view of direct marketing. If you take the AWAI course or any of the
other copywriting courses out there you get great advice and great direction
on how to write a winning promotion.
So many of the new copywriters I deal with coming out of those courses
have never been taught where the grand strategy for the promotion came
from, why the company developed this product in the first place, why they’re
not doing soft offers instead of only hard offers, why they’re not trying
continuities.
Why this media? Why direct mail when it’s so expensive? Why not try TV?
What happens to a customer after I bring him in? All of those things.
There’s a great book out there that I recommend. It’s by Bob Stone and I
think it’s updated almost every year. It’s called, Successful Direct Marketing
Methods. Amazon has it. I read an ancient version of it. I think it was in
Sanskrit back in 1973 or ’74 when I first got into this racket.

-- 20 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Everything that I learned about copywriting was within the context of the
overall strategy and nuts and bolts that I learned from studying that book. It
is a dense read and will take a long time to work your way through it and feel
that you’ve got a good grip on it. It’s a great way to get started.
Does that answer your question?
Tony: 	It does, although it raises another one.
You get started and things are going along. We’re copywriters and we sell
the best possible outcome, but not every partnership deal works out. How
do you give yourself a higher chance at succeeding in terms of that?
Clayton: 	A couple of things. First of all, when you’re discussing this with a potential
partner, you’re the copywriter and you’re going in and you really want to
develop this. When discussing this with a potential partner, humility is the
best possible way to approach it.
Rather than saying, “Boy, you just sign this deal, make me your partner, and
I going to explode your company. I’m going to quadruple your company and
I’m going to do this, that, and the other thing.”
If you do that and come in short in any of the things you just promised, you
look like a fool and a failure. Even if you produced a positive response but
not as big as you’d hoped for, the relationship is then weaker than it should
be.
However you might say, “Hey, why don’t we try this?” Kind of like I did with
SRC. I didn’t tell them I was going to give them a 1000% bump in sales. I
just simply told them I thought I could do a lot better than what was being
done now.
So first of all set the expectation at a proper place. The honest truth is your
client is intelligent or he wouldn’t have a company of his own to start with.
He knows that the success or failure of any given promotion is dependent on
a lot of things. The economy and/or the market can change in a heartbeat.
The theme that’s chosen for this particular promotion may not resonate.
I’ve been in situations where a guy took years building up his mail quantities
and then in November dropped his biggest promotion to date. He put all of
his money in the mail for a financial newsletter product.

-- 21 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
It was all about how Carter was killing the economy and you couldn’t trust
Washington to protect the value of your money. Of course that very month
Reagan was elected.
All of our prospects figured we’d elected a Messiah and they didn’t need
advice anymore because the stock market was going to go straight up. My
friend absolutely got killed in the mail. There are all kinds of variables.
You lack credibility when you make promises of doubling or quadrupling or
quintupling his file. He knows that’s unfounded promises and so avoid it like
the plague. Be credible.
Say, “I think we have a good shot at giving you a substantial increase in
response. Let me give it a shot.”
Beyond that, in terms of selecting the client in the first place there are some
things that are really important. In fact, they are absolutely essential. A lot of
these are things you wouldn’t think of off the top of your head when you’re
thinking about partnering.
The first thing is chemistry. You’ve got to like the person that’s running the
company. You’ve got to enjoy spending time with them because you’re going
to be spending a lot of time with them. They need to like you.
You know, sometimes you just look at somebody and you want to smack
them in the nose. Maybe they remind you of a cop that gave you a ticket or
something, or a mean uncle you had. I have that effect on a lot of people.
They want to smack me around.
Chemistry is important. In terms of personal chemistry, having some things
in common you can chat about beyond work is also helpful. Also when
it comes to chemistry, I think everyone on this calls knows that I have a
partnering relationship with Weiss Research.
Martin and I are polar opposites in every sense of the word. I’m a high
school dropout, he’s a Columbia Ph.D. I’m more right brain than left. I have
very creative aptitude and some accounting aptitude but I lean toward the
right brain. Martin is almost 100% right brain and extremely detail oriented.
He has flashes of brilliance on the creative side, but normal operating mode
is very left brain. He over thinks things and is very detail oriented until hell
won’t have it.

-- 22 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 3 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
That vast difference and gulf between us personality-wise has actually
proven to be a huge money maker for us. We complement each other.
It’s funny and I see this all the time where they interview these old couples
that have been married two or three hundred years. How come it worked?
“Half of all the couples in America get divorced. How come you guys stuck
together?”
More often than not one or both will say, “We’re just so different that we
complement each other. We complete each other.”
The most successful relationships I’ve had with clients have been
relationships where we completed each other on a personal level.
Number two is desire. The client has to have a fire in his belly. He’s got to
really want to do this, because it’s not going to be easy. It’s not going to
happen overnight, but he has to want to grow the company.
You’d think that’s a given, if you own a company you want to grow it.
However, I’ve had a couple of experiences otherwise; one where I partnered
with a client and his wife didn’t want to grow the company. She was happy
having a small family oriented firm, so there was a constant struggle
between my client’s wife and me.
Finally, at one point – and I don’t know what caused it – but my client simply
told his wife that she could stop coming into the office now.
He said, “I’m doing this with Clayton and then we’re going to sell the
company. Then you and I can go do whatever we want to for the rest of our
lives.” That’s how committed this guy was to growing his company.
On the other hand, I had another guy come to visit me when I lived in
Sarasota, a company owner, and I think most of the people on the call
would know the company. We were out to dinner one night and I had just
completed a promotion for this guy.
I’m looking for him to nail it big time, be aggressive, and make me lots of
royalties. At dinner he said, “You know, I don’t want to be a big company. I’m
about the size I want to be now. I’m really not interested in growth.”
There are people out there like that and it’s good to know that before you
jump into bed with them.

-- 23 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Another thing I look for is a customer list. The company needs to have a
customer list. Ideally it should be underused or poorly used, or at least show
opportunities where I can use it to better advantage.
When I say customer list I mean more than a name, email address, and
street address. I mean order history of what they bought when, and what
they responded to. Have the source where the customer originally came
from.
“Oh, well, we were in a Sharper Image catalog and this guy came in from a
promotion to that list.”
His complete order history is good. If you have that you’re well on the way
to being able to establish key metrics like customer lifetime value and to
categorize customers using the RFA code concept that we discussed earlier.
Another one is prospect universe. I want to know that the company has a
substantial enough list of prospects in the first place to warrant my going to
work with them. In other words, I can grow them that way.
I’ve written in the past about – this is new with me but a lot of other guys
have written about it. There are only a few ways to grow a company.
You can get them more customers. You can get each customer to order
more often, or can get each customer to make bigger purchases every time
he orders. Also, you can get a customer to stay with you longer.
A big part of that, of course, is having a prospect universe that’s large
enough so that you can do the first thing, which is to grow the customer
base. That can be a little tricky.
Go to the library and get the Standard Rate Data Service and look up the
prospective client’s industry. If it’s catalog sales then look at catalog sales.
If it’s newsletters look that up. Look at what all of his competitors are doing
and try to get a sense for how large the number of active customers really is
in that universe. It can change radically.
A few years ago there were probably about 10 million prospects in the
investment newsletter universe. Today I would be surprised if there were
a million. By prospects I mean people who in the last 12 months have
subscribed to an investment newsletter. That universe has shrunk notably.
Be aware of that. Be aware of how large the universe is.

-- 24 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Look at the hotline names in the SRDS (Standard Rate Data Service) for
this guy’s customers and for his company. The hotline names are the people
who bought within the last specified period of time – usually 60 to 90 days.
That tells you how quickly they’re adding customers to their file now. That
gives you a sense of their growth potential.
I mentioned earlier the company needs to have or be willing to develop a
product matrix. That includes products and offers at attractive prices for
new customers, as well as higher prices for existing customers so you can
maximize revenues.
The company needs to have sufficient infrastructure to support a marketing
push. You don’t want to be hiring list brokers, print buyers, and media buyers
and doing all that. The company should have people who can handle that for
you or be willing to provide them.
Finally, at the very least, the company should have enough capital set aside
to sustain operations for a reasonable period of time. That does sound like a
no-brainer, but you’d be surprised.
I had meetings last year with a company that looked really great. They
had a very small cash crisis. I guess to most of us it would seem small for
a company. I think they had somebody reneged on a $70,000 debt to the
company and it almost put them out of business. It froze their marketing.
The company doesn’t have to have a large war chest. That’s cool. If they
have a customer file, especially if it has not been fully exploited, that’s
money in the bank. That’s money that you can extract in 30 days.
All it takes is a well crafted customer file promotion. It can be a campaign or
a single hit and you can generally pull big money out of a customer file that
can finance your marketing going forward.
I told this story before – again. I’ll hold it to three on this one. This is my Dan
Rosenthal story. Dan came into a company that was one of the smaller to
midsize financial publishers.
He says, “Tell you what. You guys have 25,000 or 30,000 paying customers,
actives right now. You’re just not promoting anything to these people after
the fact.
“You’re making all your money on renewals but you’re really not offering any

-- 25 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
other products to these people. I want to offer them a $5,000 service.”
Now these are people that spent $99 to subscribe to a newsletter. So Dan
is saying I want to offer them a $5,000 product that will be a trading product
– trade options and interest rate options.
The guy agrees, Dan writes the promotion, and they put it in the mail. About
a week later they’re counting $10 million in sales. This is out of a file of less
than 30,000 names in less than 30 days.
If you’re a copywriter marketer and you walk into a company and you see
a customer list that’s under or poorly used, not fully leveraged – basically a
virgin list is what Dan walked into in this particular deal. You take just 10% of
the revenues, that’s $1 million dollars in 30 days.
More importantly the company now has $9 million that it’s sitting on now.
In this case the fulfillment of the product was online so there was really no
fulfillment cost. That was pretty much net money.
Now the company had $9 million in its war chest to do any darn thing it
wanted to in terms of extracting new customers.
That is really the goal. If you can find a good customer list that has been
under utilized, you don’t have to have a lot of capital in the company. You
need just enough to make sure they can continue operations. You can strike
gold with that customer list.
Tony: 	What you are describing is obviously a royalty or percentage deal. How do
you trust that somebody’s actually going to pay you? Let’s say you make
somebody a couple of million dollars doing that and then what’s to prevent
them from not saying, “See ya! I didn’t say that”?
Clayton: 	First of all you can’t trust a contract to help you, not with that anyway. There
are a lot of great things a contract can do for you, but frankly any contract is
only as good as the character of the counter parties that sign it.
Admittedly I tend to focus in a couple of niches and these are highly
incestuous niches. I know all the successful copywriters in the health and
investment newsletter and supplement markets.
I know the major players. There are some of the major players that I wouldn’t
work with if you held a gun to my head. The reason is I have heard from
copywriters that they reneged, cheated, or took short cuts around paying

-- 26 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
royalties.
There are ways to do that. They are not transparent. Copywriters figure it out
and word gets out. Before you know it that particular guy is having trouble
finding top quality talent. Word does get around.
If you’re in a different niche that is not as incestuous, what I would suggest is
that when you’re having your first discussions with the company owner that
you find subtle ways to find out who he’s worked with before.
I love to have dinner with the client before I go into the office and meet his
people. After I’ve kind of prequalified him on the phone and long distance,
I’ll jump on a plane. They’ll generally agree to pick up your expenses. Zip on
out there for some face-to-face time.
I like meeting with the client over dinner the night before I go into the office.
Just having a nice relaxed time and talk a little bit about his history, his past,
and what he’s experienced. Part of that is finding out who he’s worked with
before.
Then it’s a simple matter of when you get home to ring some of those people
up and talk to them about, “Have you worked with this guy before? Was he
fair with you? Did he pay his bills? Did he pay on time? Was he right on the
money with the royalties?” and those kinds of financial questions.
First of all, establish that you’re talking to somebody who is trustworthy in
those ways. Frankly, a smart client will overpay a copywriter marketer that
he values, if anything, because he understands that if you have a dispute,
even if you’re right, if you have a dispute with the copywriter and the
copywriter goes on down the road, that word gets around fast and it’s going
to be hard to get good talent in the future.
The other thing is I require daily promotion reports. If you’re doing direct
mail, the reports don’t have to be daily, but I’m doing direct mail as well as
daily web-based promotions. I ask for a daily promotion report that shows
me what the response-rate, average sale, and total amount of dollars is on
every promotion that we’re doing, every promotion that’s active.
I have one client that sends me those promotions four times a day. He’s
automated the process. I know what his cancels are, what his refund
requests are, how many people ordered today, how many people cancelled,
what our total revenues are, the whole nine yards.

-- 27 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 8 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Now this is not something I ask for to keep the client honest. This is
something I ask for because I need this data in order to suggest strategies
and tactics and so forth to improve his response and average sale down
the road and see how people are responding to different kinds of offers, to
monitor our test panels.
With web marketing, where you’re emailing to a file every single day or six
days a week, as we often do, it allows you to make mid-course corrections
on the fly. If a particular appeal or offer isn’t working, you don’t have to wait
some period of time in order to know that. You know it within hours. You’re
able to maybe do a flight of real quick tests or do something else to change
the direction of the campaign to maximize response and sales. That’s how I
do it.
The bottom line is, as I said before, it’s in the client’s best interest to make
you very happy if you’re producing huge – I mean, imagine that client when
Rosenthal goes in and produces $10 million in a week, in a promotion that
ran over a week. I think it took him like a week or ten days to write it, and the
promotion itself just lasted a week, and he produces $10 million.
Imagine that client cheaping out on Rosenthal at that point. This guy’s just
handed you $9 million. At that point, if you get cheap with say the 10%
royalty – I think Dan got more than that -- let’s say at a 10% commission on
sales, you cheap out on that $1 million and it’s going to cost you the next
$10 million he could have produced for you. It would be nuts to do that. In
my experience, very few people do.
Tony: 	Okay. Another thing that I wanted to cover was I’m very detail-oriented,
and I really want to give people the complete picture, and I know that not
everything is perfect and utopian with this model.
So if there’s anything else that you can think of in terms of issues or pitfalls
or things that somebody that’s getting into this has to watch for or problems
that they may have to address, I would love to hear that.
Clayton: 	Sure. Well, there are a lot of things to watch out for. I’ve been burned by all
of these at one point or another, but if there’s a change in ownership at a
company, put on your running shoes and be ready to bolt. It doesn’t mean
you have to, but it means those things just generally don’t work out well.
Change in management – a lot of times you’ll take a small company, make
it big, and the owner will say, “Okay, now I want a CEO so I don’t have to do
the day-to-day stuff.”

-- 28 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page  of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
That’s always a red flag because the new management also quite often
doesn’t work out well. You may find an “us versus them” mentality among
the client staff where they resist ideas from outside. They want to run the
show. You threaten their position so an adversarial relationship arises.
A change in the regulatory environment or compliance regulations at a
company is going to signal a change in response rates. That gets my
attention.
The company is experiencing legal problems, not just a trivial lawsuit or
something like that but you find out the company is being investigated or
accused of doing something grossly wrong. That is another red flag to look
out for.
A year before 9/11, I was sitting at the top of the World Trade Center in New
York, almost to the day, being grilled by The Commodity Futures Trading
Commission because I had accepted three assignments from a newsletter
editor who turned out to be one of the biggest scam artists and scum bags
I’d ever heard of, but I didn’t know that at the time I accepted the work.
It ended up costing me six weeks of work and $20,000 in legal fees just to
get through the deposition. Legal problems can be a biggie. There’s a lot you
can do to cover yourself there with contracts. We’re not going to have time
for that today.
By the way, we’re an hour and 20 minutes into this hour-long call, but I say
we just keep going.
Tony: 	Yeah, I still have a few more questions I’d like to get handled.
Clayton: 	Okay, great.
Tony: 	If we could. If you’re up for it, I am.
Clayton: 	Yeah, I’m cool.
Tony: 	Okay.
Clayton: 	Might as well get all these thoughts out, right?
Tony: 	Yep.

-- 29 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 30 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Clayton: 	Okay. A change in market psychology is also a big red flag. We saw that
happen in the investments markets in about 2002. We had the tech wreck
and all of that kind of mess and then we had 9/11 and anthrax in the mail.
Suddenly our 10 million strong prospect universe shrunk to about 1 million.
People just decided they didn’t need investment newsletters anymore. A
change in market psychology is a big sign of danger.
A change in the product quality or becoming aware that the product was
never quite what you assumed it was in terms of quality; all of those things
are dangerous signs to look out for.
Having said all of that, if an owner decides to sell, none of your business,
they’re going to do it whether you like it or not.
If they decide that they want new management or if there’s a change in the
regulatory environment or if they have legal problems, there’s nothing you
can do about those things.
The one thing you can deal with is the “us versus them” mentality on the
client’s staff. That is very common. You can deal with that.
The best way I know to deal with the kind of sniping and sometimes
sabotage that will happen as staff members attempt to prove that you’re no
damn good and that the company doesn’t need you – and it will happen, it
does happen because people are threatened by outside experts – the best
way to deal with that is to not allow it to happen quietly in the background
and to bring it out into the sunlight the first time it happens and every time it
happens.
Have a little chat on the phone with the person and with the owner and just
deal with it like an adult. It’s amazing how quickly it stops when you do that.
Tony: 	How helpful is it to befriend those people in those kind of – I know some
people obviously are just belligerent or they have issues and they’re not
going to come around, but have you found it valuable to make friends with
people other than the owner – other than your primary contact –to be almost
like a politician a little bit in a sense, kind of make your agenda get pushed
through more smoothly?
Clayton: 	Yeah, you really need to. Whenever I’m doing these deals, I insist first of
all that my primary contact is the company owner and that strategic issues
are discussed between the two of us. In some cases, he’ll want his general
manager involved in this or he’ll want another person also there and that’s

-- 30 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 3 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
cool.
That’s fine so long as the company owner is on board and that you guys
agree on the strategy and the priority list and the way that you’re going to
address things. It doesn’t matter who else is on the call. That’s all fine and
good.
The second thing though is that sometimes I’ve had it happen where
the company owner and I would spend our time together and lay out the
strategy and then he would go back and tell the staff and they’d all be upset
because they weren’t involved in the decision-making.
Again, giving some staff members the opportunity to be involved in those
strategy meetings is important.
Secondly, urging the client to communicate with his staff is important.
Third, making it clear from the outside with the company owner that you
want to communicate with his staff about these things is important. When
you do that, it’s important that you not force feed them or make a scene that
they’re being force-fed.
I’ll give you a good example. We have a major push going on right now
that involves campaigns for seven analysts with a gauntlet series including
videos and free reports and all kinds of things for seven analysts and then
ultimately a sale as part of this gauntlet.
Again, a gauntlet is the strategy that you use to handle and to create
bonding and hopefully also create a secondary sale in a new lead or in a
new customer’s first 30 or 60 days on file.
This is, as you can imagine with seven of these campaigns going, being
created at the same time, getting the strategy right, and getting everyone on
board.
There’s a heavy web component. There’s a direct mail component. There’s a
video component, so all of the right people had to be on board with this.
So I did a webinar. I prepared this PowerPoint presentation on “Here’s
what Martin and I’ve been talking about and here’s how we kind of see it
happening, and the purpose of this thing is to get your input, your ideas to
identify things that can’t be done or we’ll have to get additional resources to
do and to just get your general opinion on it.”

-- 31 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 3 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
It works great. Everyone gets to contribute to the process and everyone’s
on board, plus everyone knows which direction you’re going, which always
helps. Bringing the staff into the process is extremely important.
Tony: 	I see. A couple times right there you mentioned the strategy, and you and
I both know that the best company in the world can, a lot of times, fail if
there’s a faulty strategy behind it ,whereas an average company, a lot of
times, with a great strategy will make the client a lot of money.
How do you ensure that you and the client are on the same page when it
comes to strategy and really the execution of that strategy?
Clayton: 	I’m kind of linear in the way that I think. I kind of structure it in a way that
makes sense for me, and the thing that’s worked best for me over the years
is that I attempt quarterly face-to-face meetings with the client.
I live up here in the boonies in Western North Carolina and my biggest
client’s in Florida and I have clients in several other states, I try to have face-
to-face strategy meetings with those clients once a quarter.
Sometimes it’s once every five months or four months, but fairly regularly
during the year, and the timing is often suggested by a major promotion or
new product that we’re launching or whatever.
Those quarterly face-to-face meetings are very important. They’re important
for political reasons, as well just to make sure everyone’s on the same page.
Secondly, I have regular telephone conferences that are scheduled, so
many times a week or so many times a month on a particular day at a
particular time and those times are inviolate. They happen no matter what
else. There could be an earthquake in California and a tornado in North
Carolina and somehow we’d still have it.
It’s absolutely essential to respond to new opportunities, new challenges,
changes in the market and so forth. Really stay on the same page, stay in
touch. I’ve become a Skype addict lately. I love Skype. I have it running all
the time.
Tony: 	I see Rich Schefren buzzing you on your Skype all the time when I come
into your office.
Clayton: 	Yeah, he’s like a little bunny rabbit. He pops up every 50 minutes and goes,

-- 32 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 33 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
“You there? You there?”
Skype is great because I have my clients all on it and I’m writing something
and I’m stuck or I have a question or I have an idea that I want to kind of get
off script on and all I have to do is type a quick Skype chat to the guy, and
I’ve got my answer in a matter of seconds. I use a telephone and Skype very
heavily that way.
Email is probably the worst. Anytime I can avoid email when talking to a
client, I do. It drives me crazy when people are constantly using email and
then the person doesn’t get back to them.
Then you say, “What did you find out about this?”
“Well, I emailed him.”
“When?”
“Two weeks ago.”
“And you haven’t heard back?”
“No.”
“You see this thing on your desk that we paid a lot of money for? It’s got
all the buttons on it? Pick up the damn phone. Call him. Rattle their cage.
We’ve got to get this done.”
Email is a problem because I think it actually extends the amount of time
it takes to get some things done, but also because it quite often can be
taken in the worst possible way and create political problems in a client’s
organization.
You might say something perfectly innocently and have somebody read it
and feel it’s a personal attack on them simply because you copied a couple
of people that they’re sensitive to.
Now email I try to avoid unless I’m trying to create a permanent record of
a particular communication. I try to use the more spontaneous ways of
communicating.
Tony: 	Okay. Another thing that I’ve observed, and I’m sure people on our list have
as well, is that a lot of times there are companies with a great product, a

-- 33 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 3 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
product that the market is hot for, the product is really good and they’re in
need of marketing, but at the same time also their company is just a total
mess.
Maybe they have bad management, lousy employees; just their level of
organization and in terms of their systems and everything that’s going on is
way off. Are there situations where you try to fix that -- go in and fix all that
other stuff on top of the marketing -- or do you just –
Clayton: 	No, I’ve done it. I’ve done it but it just depends. If the company is a lost
cause, I wouldn’t have accepted them in the first place. Remember the
beginning of the process for me is that face-to-face meeting so I would have
determined early on that the company was a basket case and was itself an
obstacle to creating a success.
Sometimes a product is just so good and the opportunity seems so huge.
Maybe it’s a huge prospect universe or they got a big list or there’s some
other reason why you really, really want to do this, but the company is a
basket case and they really need major help on every level.
I dive in with both feet, if the money is there. If the potential for growth is
there and so strong that I just can’t resist temptation, what can I do? I’m
going to have to do it.
I would never begin by going in and trying to turn their world upside down.
I’d much rather have it happen kind of naturally as the relationship deepens,
because it’s like a car.
A particular old rattle trap might do just great at 35 mph just kind of plugging
along, but the minute you put your foot on the carburetor and try to get some
real performance out of that thing and take it up to 80-90 mph, all of the
sudden the tires are shaking off the rims and the doors are rattling and the
engine’s coughing. Because you’re putting the machine under stress, the
flaws in the machine are starting to make themselves evident.
Once you get involved with a company and you begin exploding that
company’s revenues and profits, increasing the number of promotions going
out and doing those things, those cracks become glaringly obvious.
You may have spotted them before, but now the company owner is spotting
them himself, and so instead of feeling he has to defend his staff or his
organizational structure, or his internal procedures, or his infrastructure in
terms of IT or software whatever – instead of feeling like you’re attacking

-- 34 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 3 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
and criticizing and he’s defending, now he’s saying, “Holy cow man, we’ve
got to fix this. This is costing us money.”
Again, the watchword is, “Let it happen naturally.” Go in, do your job, write
great sales copy, come up with great strategies, and then as the company
begins accelerating in its growth cycle, then these things will become
obvious.
The owner is going to be coming to you and saying, “How do we fix this?
There were 27 typos in that last email. How do I make sure that doesn’t
happen? They sent it to the wrong file segment. We got a new control
in January, now it’s March and we’re still not out with the roll out. What
procedures do we need in place? What people do we need in place to be
able to increase the number of mailings we do per year?”
Those kinds of things can happen naturally. Be sensitive to the fact that the
company is your client’s baby. Although he’s frustrated with the growth of
the company and thinks he’s got the best product in the world and the world
should be beating a path to his door, the fact of the matter is he built that
company, he selected those people.
One of the things I hear most often, and it drives me crazy just because
everyone says it is that, “We’re a family. It’s not a company. We’re a little
family. We’re all involved in each other’s lives. We’re all best friends.”
The minute you have to cut back staff and one of your best friends has to
be laid off; I’ll bet you it will be 20 years before you ever hear from them
again. They’re not your best friends. They’re not your family. They’re your
employees.
There’s this sense that in small companies, “Oh, we’re all just a little
family.” You go in and you start telling this guy that his family is a bunch of
incompetent miscreants and you’re not going to get a warm reception, so
just go ahead and let it happen naturally.
Tony: 	I see. It seems like all the marketing stuff and the copywriting stuff itself is
already a lot of work potentially. There seems like there’s really no limit.
Potentially, you could just keep going and going.
That’s another concern I have is as the work level grows and the work load
grows I think even beyond – if it’s succeeding and they’re making more
money and they want to grow and grow, how do you manage all the work?
Do you begin to outsource some of it that you know that it would be better

-- 35 of 38 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 1 • Introduction
Transcript 	Page 3 of 38
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
suited for somebody else to be doing it?
Clayton: 	Again, it’s pretty natural if you just let it happen. You start off by doing what
you can do. At first, there’ll be plenty to do. You’ll have to prioritize the work
and you work your way through it and you’ll start to see sales taking off like
crazy, and then it will get to the point where there’s just too much for one
person to do.
I had that happen with a client. It was with Weiss in the early 2000s. That’s
how I ended up mentoring Parris Lampropoulos and Carline Anglade-Cole
and Kent Komae and Bob Hutchinson and Brad Petersen.
